[[RunningHead]]

Current Concepts of the Psychoanalytic Process

Martin Stein M.D. and Stephen Morgenstern M.D.

Stephen Morgenstern

Martin H. Stein, M.D.
Stephen A. Morgenstern, M.D.
1023 Indian Creek Road
Philadelphia, Pennsylvania 1915

Stephen Morgenstern  

This panel, originally intended to consider the "therapeutic process," underwent a change in title and direction as it shifted to a consideration of the "psychoanalytic process." Martin Stein, in his opening remarks, commented on this shift from an emphasis on cure to a focus on the analytic work itself. Examining the title further, he emphasized that all that is current is not new. Our long-established concepts of conflict, resistance, re-experiencing of the past, and ego dominance are all current. Yet, they have been reinterpreted through the years. Each interpretation may add to theory, for theory-building depends upon a process of historical accretion, an encompassing of various points of view in order to establish new and more useful hypotheses.

In the initial formal presentation, Roy Schafter demonstrated this concept as he both clarified our older formulations and suggested modifications in them. Three interrelated theses define for Schafer the psychoanalytic process. It is, first, the construction of a certain kind of life history; it is, second, a certain kind of present subjective world; and it leads to a transformation of the terms in which an analysand defines and understands his or her past and present. A Freudian life history is one of the many possible life histories. In it, one deals with versions of the major typical sexual and aggressive conflicts of childhood.

Held at the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York City, December, 1974. Panelists: Manuel Furer, Samuel D. Lipton, Roy Schafter.

Submitted May 13, 1975

181

Effects on Adults of Object Loss in the First Five Years

Justin Call M.D. and Martha Wolfenstein Ph.D.

Martha Wolfenstein

Justin D. Call, M.D.
Martha Wolfenstein, Ph.D.
Albert Einstein College of Medicine
Rousso Building
1165 Morris Park Avcnue
Bronx, N.Y. 10461

Martha Wolfenstein  

Dr. Justin Call, organizer and chairman of the panel, opened the panel by speaking of the importance of recognizing the long-term effects of early object loss. Referring to Freud's "Mourning and Melancholia," he remarked that, when an object toward whom there is strong ambivalence is lost, the object becomes absorbed into the ego, while the hostility toward the object is then turned against the ego. In "The Ego and the Id" Freud speaks of identification as the regular sequel to loss of a love object so that, over time, the ego is repeatedly altered by assuming aspects of different objects loved and lost. Freud also speaks of a sequence of traumata occurring in the course of development, each of which re-evokes those preceding it. Call considered that successive object relations could be similarly qualified, the course of later relationships recapitulating the vicissitudes of earlier ones.

Call alluded briefly to a case of a chronically depressed young man in his thirties whose father had died when he was four and a half and whose mother had then become depressed. The patient did not acknowledge the father's death, even though he had a clear memory of watching the father's burial. The patient had a persisting expectation that his father would return through earthquakes, storms, from the depths of the great ocean, in whirlpools, and through some religious mystical power. He also looked to other men to re-enact early memories and fantasies he had of himself with the father. He fantasied his father with an all-providing penis, infusing needed supplies into him through his anus.

Held at the Annual Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Los Angeles, May, 1975. Panelists: Allan Compton, Irving Kaufman, Thomas Mintz, Peter B. Neubauer.

Submitled December 17, 1975

[[RunningHead]]

Adolescent To Woman

Samuel Ritvo M.D.

Samuel Ritvo

Samuel Ritvo, M.D.
Yale University Child Study Center
333 Cedar Street
New Haven, Connecticut 06510

Samuel Ritvo  

HOW DOES ADOLESCENCE BEGIN and how does it end? We can take puberty as a starting point because the psychological changes of adolescence have to be understood in relation to the biology of puberty. The changes in the body that accompany sexual development and reproductive maturity force a change in the image of the body with far-reaching psychological consequences. With the body changes comes an intensification in sexual and aggressive urges which have little alternative but to come into unwelcome connection with the infantile and incestuous objects. The ties to the infantile objects have to be loosened and shifted to someone in the peer generation. When the adolescent girl loosens the tie to her parents, she loses the support and organization she has drawn from having the parent's ego, ego ideal, and superego as auxiliaries to her own (Lampl-de Groot, 1960). In this vulnerable state the adolescent upheaval, which is necessary and even desirable for the deepenin of the personality and for eventual adult independence, is strongly influenced by the history of the childhood conflicts — how they were resolved, what propensity to regression remains and to which early fixation points — what favorite defenses were instituted against anxiety, what character traits were formed. In assessing adolescents, we must look, too, for their progressive, creative potential, their ability to find new solutions for both old and new problems.

The Faust Legend Seen in the Light of an Analytic Case

David Werman M.D. and John Rhoads M.D.

John Rhoads

David S. Werman, M.D.
John M. Rhoads, M.D.
The University of North Carolina School of Medicine Department of Psychiatry
Chapel Hill, North Carolina 27514

John Rhoads  

SUMMARY

The Faust legend has an ancient history and has formed the basis of folktales, myths, poems, plays, and novels. We have characterized the Faust figure as one who strives intensely for omnipotence, omniscience, and boundless sexual gratification, and magically seeks to fulfill these goals through contracts,

120

or pacts, that he makes with a god or a devil. Whereas the psychogenetic background of such individuals may be very different and the complex described here is but a "final common pathway" through which various psychodynamic mechanisms are represented and expressed, we believe at least one of these psychodynamic configurations has been delineated and some additional light cast on the Faust figure.

[[RunningHead]]

Minutes of the Vienna Psychoanalytic Society, Volume III: 1910-1911

Edited by Herman Nunberg and Ernst Federn. Translated by M. Nunberg with the assistance of H. Collins. New York: International Universities Press, 1974, xviii + 367 pp., $20.00.

Ernest Wolf M.D.

Ernest Wolf

Ernest S. Wolf, M.D.
Chicago, Illinois

Ernest Wolf  

Lean objectivity barely veils the dramatic struggle reported in this third volume of the Minutes of the Vienna Psychoanalytic Society as Freud tries to safeguard the "wonderful diversity of psychology" by purging Adler and his followers from the founder's inner circle. This book, like the two volumes that preceded it, is both a historical documentation of the earliest years of an organized psychoanalytic movement and, at the same time, a window onto the early development

683

of psychoanalytic thought as it emerged from the dialogue of Freud and his circle of collaborators, admirers, critics, detractors, and just plain hangers-on.

Organized psychoanalysis owes it origins to Freud's increasing sense of isolation during the months when the bonds of friendship with Fliess grew more tenuous. In the fall of 1902 Freud invited Adler, Kahane, Reitler, and Stekel to meet regularly with him at his house for the discussion of his work. The "Psychological Wednesday Society" soon took in additional members, and from 1906 until 1915 Otto Rank, as the official secretary of the Society, recorded the minutes. In 1908 a reorganization resulted in the more formal designation of "Vienna Psychoanalytic Society," and after 1910 the meetings were moved from Freud's private home to larger quarters. World War I brought its difficulties, not the least of which was that Rank had to join the military service in 1915. After Rank's departure, the recorded minutes became very fragmentary and there are no known plans for their publication. The present volume, the third in the series, with one more to follow, reports on 40 meetings from October 1910 to December 1911. The minutes for the meeting of 31 May, 1911, the last of the 1910-1911 season, are missing. One cannot help but be intrigued by this singular omission, inasmuch as only two weeks later Freud wrote Jung that he finally got rid of Adler (who had normally participated and had shown no signs of wishing to resign as late as the meeting on the 24th of May).

The Schreber Case: Psychoanalytic Profile of a Paranoid Personality

By William G. Niederland. New York: Quandrangle/The New York Times Book Co., 1974, XVI + 172 pp., $8.95.

Nathaniel London M.D.

Nathaniel London

Nathaniel J. London, M.D.
Minneapolis, Minn.

Nathaniel London  

"Wer Kennt schon den Dr. Schreber?" (Who, after all, knows Dr. Schreber?), as Niederland informs us, was the scornful headline with which a Saxony newspaper greeted Daniel Paul Schreber's candidacy for the Reichstag in 1884. This eminent jurist lost the election and was subsequently hospitalized for six months for a serious mental breakdown. After he was promoted to presiding judge, Court of Appeals, in 1893, he became even more severely disabled by mental illness. While his legal and political achievements have been blurred by the passage of time, he remains deservedly well recognized in psychoanalysis and psychiatry because of the scientific value of the Memoirs of his mental illness (1903).1 Those times were ripe for a redefinition of mental illness, and the openness and explicit detail of Schreber's report foreshadowed the impressive advances of psychiatry in this century. However, the Memoirs would hardly be known today had not Freud used them in 1911 for his most important contribution to the psychoanalytic exploration of paranoid states and schizophrenia: "Psycho-analytic Notes on an Autobiographical Account of a Case of Paranoia."

Freud's work is best known outside of psychoanalysis because it provided a means of understanding the hitherto enigmatic delusional thinking of psychotics in psychodynamic terms. Within the psychoanalytic community, Freud's Schreber study is even more significant because it marked a turning point in the early development of psychoanalysis. It is basic to the two principal theories of schizophrenia in contemporary psychoanalysis.2 Even more significant for our field, the Schreber "Notes" mark the origin of Freud's formulation and study of narcissism and object relations. He extended his psychic energy/libido hypotheses in order to organize these new findings, and revisions of these theories constitute a major issue for psychoanalysis today.

[[RunningHead]]

Schizophrenia and the Need-Fear Dilemma

By D. L. Burnham, A. I Gladstone and R. W. Gibson. New York: International Universities Press, 1969, XV + 474 pp., $15.00.

Peter Hartocollis M.D.

Peter Hartocollis

Peter Hartocollis, M.D.
C. F. Menninger Memorial Hospital

Peter Hartocollis  

Respectable as it has become again in recent years, the notion of schizophrenia is acceptable to most of us, clinicians and researchers in the field of mental illness, as something of a compromise. As Burnham, author of the theoretical chapters of this book points out, schizophrenia is but a conventional label, a diagnostic umbrella for "diverse and far-reaching" phenomena, difficult if at all possible to account for by means of unitary theory. Burnham and his collaborators actually prefer to speak of "the schizophrenic person" rather than of "schizophrenia," making use of the more operational, Meyerian notion of a "schizophrenic reaction" or "disorganization," which official psychiatry decided to do away with not too long ago.1

Burnham's thesis is that a deficient psychological differentiation and integration in early life results in a vulnerable personality structure, one that predisposes to a schizophrenic reaction. According to this thesis, the person who is predisposed to such a reaction suffers from an "inordinate need and fear of the influence of other persons"—a "dilemma" that not only creates a morbid predisposition, but also accounts for the specificity of the psychotic reaction.

1I understand that the committee currently at work to revise APA's Diagnostic & Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM-II) is in favor of changing again the over-all label, this time from "Schizophrenia" to "Schizophrenic Disorders."714
